evals.name: assumption-map-build
evals.prompt: I'm building an AI-powered contract review tool for solo attorneys. My hypothesis is that solo attorneys spend 3+ hours reviewing a standard commercial lease and would pay $99/month for a tool that cuts review time by 70%. I've done 6 discovery interviews but no one has paid yet. Build me a complete assumption map.
evals.expected_output: A three-tier assumption map (existential, serious, important) covering customer, problem, solution, business model, and technical assumptions — with cheapest test design for each and a prioritized test sequence.
expectations: Assumptions are organized into three tiers (TIER 1 existential, TIER 2 serious, TIER 3 important) — tiering forces the founder to focus on assumptions that could kill the business before optimizing ones that merely matter
Each assumption has an ID (A-001, A-002...), risk level, evidence status, and cheapest test design — without test designs, the assumption map is a worry list rather than an action plan

The willingness-to-pay assumption ($99/month from solo attorneys) is flagged as TIER 1 existential — solo practitioners are price-sensitive and the assumption that they will pay $99/month recurring is the riskiest business model assumption
Customer, problem, solution, business model, and technical categories are all covered — incomplete category coverage leaves blind spots where untested assumptions hide
The cheapest test for each assumption follows the MVT hierarchy (conversation before landing page before MVP) — the goal is to invalidate bad ideas cheaply, not to build first and learn later
A prioritized test sequence is provided (Week 1-2 for zero-cost tests, Week 3-4 for prototype tests) — without sequencing, founders test what's fun rather than what's risky

evals.name: mvp-design-scoping
evals.prompt: Based on my assumption map, my top 3 assumptions to test are: (1) solo attorneys will pay $99/month for contract review, (2) the AI can achieve 90% accuracy on standard commercial lease clauses, (3) attorneys will trust AI-flagged issues enough to skip full manual review. I have a 2-person team and 8 weeks. Design the minimum viable product.
evals.expected_output: An MVP design document with minimum feature set (each feature mapped to an assumption), explicitly excluded features, specific success/failure criteria, and an 8-week build plan.
expectations: Every feature in the MVP maps to at least one of the 3 critical assumptions — features that don't test a critical assumption are scope creep and must be excluded
An 'Explicitly Excluded' list is present and at least as long as the included feature list — the exclusion list is what makes the MVP minimum; without it, scope creeps back in
Success criteria are specific and measurable (e.g., '3 of 5 pilot attorneys pay after free trial' not 'good adoption') — vague criteria let founders declare victory regardless of actual results

Failure criteria are defined with pivot triggers (e.g., 'if 0 of 5 pay after trial: assumption A-001 is invalidated, consider freemium or different customer segment') — failure criteria are what prevent persevering with a dead idea
The 8-week build plan is realistic for a 2-person team — overscoped MVPs that take 6 months defeat the purpose of minimum viable testing
The trust assumption (attorneys trusting AI-flagged issues) is tested through the MVP design, not assumed away — this is often the hardest assumption in AI products and must be explicitly tested

evals.name: negative-idea-generation
evals.prompt: I don't have a product idea yet. Can you help me brainstorm 100 ideas for legal tech products? I want a full idea sprint with categories.
evals.expected_output: The agent should redirect this to the idea skill, since idea generation is outside the hypothesis skill's scope.
expectations: The agent recognizes this is an idea generation request and redirects to the idea skill — hypothesis is for assumption mapping and MVP design, not idea brainstorming
The agent does NOT produce a list of product ideas within the hypothesis skill — doing so would bypass the idea skill's 100-idea sprint format and DVF scoring framework

evals.name: negative-pilot-results
evals.prompt: We ran our MVP for 6 weeks with 5 pilot attorneys. 2 paid, 1 churned in week 3, 2 never converted from free trial. Can you analyze these results and tell me if I should pivot?
evals.expected_output: The agent should redirect this to the validate skill, since pilot results analysis and pivot decisions are outside the hypothesis skill's scope.
expectations: The agent recognizes this is a pilot results analysis request and redirects to the validate skill — hypothesis is for pre-build assumption mapping, not post-pilot analysis
The agent does NOT produce a BML analysis or pivot recommendation within the hypothesis skill — doing so would bypass the validate skill's evidence quality standards and pivot decision framework
